metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Capture the 'domain rubric lives in the overlay, not the verifier' architectural decision in a durable location (e.g. CLAUDE.md, an ADR, or a header comment in code-review-verify.md) so future contributors discover and respect the boundary.
findings.finding: The 'locked principle' that domain rubric lives in the overlay (not the generic verifier) is a novel architectural contract, but the plan does not call for capturing it in an ADR, CLAUDE.md, or persistent design doc. Future contributors editing code-review-verify.md may not discover this boundary rule and silently re-introduce domain logic into the verifier.
findings.location: ## What — 'locked principle that domain rubric lives in the overlay, not the generic Pass-2 verifier' / AC line 3
findings.suggested_fix: Add a brief ADR entry or a prominent comment block at the top of code-review-verify.md (e.g. `# ARCH: Domain-specific recall patterns belong in overlay files (code-review-*.md), not here`) and reference it from CLAUDE.md if one exists.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either cite the existing document that establishes the 'locked principle' (overlay vs verifier separation) or add a step to this plan to record it in an ADR, CLAUDE.md, or inline comment in code-review-verify.md.
findings.finding: The plan invokes a 'locked principle that domain rubric lives in the overlay, not the generic Pass-2 verifier.' This architectural constraint is asserted as already locked/established, but no ADR, CLAUDE.md entry, or architectural document in the repo codifies it as such. The constraint cannot be verified as 'locked' from the codebase.
findings.location: ## What — 'per the locked principle that domain rubric lives in the overlay, not the generic Pass-2 verifier'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the FP-discrimination guards (the 'both halves') in the plan — at minimum list the guard categories (e.g., one-time scripts, micro-benchmarks run offline, intentional eager-load, framework-managed caching) so the eval fixtures can be grounded against the actual guard set rather than invented by the implementer.
findings.finding: The plan states 'both halves' of FP-discrimination guards without enumerating what those guards are; an implementing agent must invent the FP-guard list from scratch, which is a design decision that directly determines whether AC2 ('does not over-suppress valid patterns') can be verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the FP-discrimination guard conditions explicitly in the plan (or reference a named, locatable source document) — specifying what property of a change causes each guard to suppress a false positive.
findings.finding: The FP-discrimination guards ('both halves') are referenced as a required deliverable in both the What section and AC-1, but the plan never defines what conditions constitute a valid FP guard, what the suppression decision rule is, or where the guard vocabulary comes from — leaving the entire FP half of AC-1 as an undefined protocol.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the FP-discrimination guards (e.g., 'only flag if the code executes in a loop', 'skip if result is immediately discarded') the same way the 8 recall patterns are listed, so the executor has no design decisions to make.
findings.finding: The FP-discrimination guards ('both halves') are never enumerated; an executing agent must invent the guard list from scratch, which is a real design decision deferred to the executor.
findings.location: Plan body / AC line 1: 'performance overlay carries both recall patterns and FP-discrimination guards'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a bullet list of FP-guard conditions analogous to the 8-pattern list, e.g.: (1) skip if the hotspot is outside a request/response path; (2) skip if collection is bounded by a compile-time constant; (3) skip if async boundary is already present; etc.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Identify and cite the concrete file or document that is the authoritative source for the 8 AI-advantaged performance patterns (the 'dso content'), or explicitly note that the taxonomy is being authored from first principles in this task.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'Enrich the performance overlay prompt with dso content' treating 'dso content' as a known, locatable source, but no canonical dso performance rubric exists in this repo. The only DSO performance content found is `docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/criteria/dso-criteria-specs.md` line 37 (T5a — a plan-review criterion, not a code-review pattern set). The specific 8-pattern taxonomy named in the plan (N+1 in loops, unbounded accumulation, blocking ops in async, over-fetch, cache stampede, needless materialization, pool misuse, non-linear complexity on user input) appears nowhere in the repo as a pre-existing 'dso' artifact.
findings.location: ## What — 'Enrich the performance overlay prompt with dso content'
findings.suggested_fix: If the source is `docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/criteria/dso-criteria-specs.md` line 37 (T5a), say so explicitly. If it is an external artifact outside this repo, specify its path or URL so the implementing agent can locate it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the 'dso content' source to a concrete, locatable artifact (a file path, ADR, URL, or inline quote) so the executor can verify the 8 recall patterns and FP-guards against it rather than reconstructing them from memory.
findings.finding: The plan's authoritative content source — 'dso content' — is never identified as a concrete, locatable artifact. A codebase-wide search finds no module, file, ADR, doc, or config named or abbreviated 'dso'; only a single unrelated subprocess-timeout test uses the string. The executor is expected to derive 8 AI-advantaged recall patterns and unspecified FP-guards from this source, but cannot locate or verify it.
findings.location: What section: 'Enrich the performance overlay prompt with dso content: recall patterns AND FP-guards (both halves)'
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) add a `## Source` section to the plan citing the exact file path or URL of the 'dso' document, or (b) inline the authoritative list of 8 patterns and their FP-guards directly in the plan body, eliminating the unresolvable reference.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate all 8 AI-advantaged recall patterns explicitly in `code-review-performance.md` with enough specificity that an executor implementing the prompt needs no additional design decisions.
findings.finding: The 8 AI-advantaged recall patterns named in the plan's What section (blocking ops in async, cache stampede, needless materialization, pool misuse, non-linear complexity on user input, over-fetch, unbounded accumulation, plus N+1 in loops) are referenced as the enrichment content but are never defined or enumerated in the overlay prompt — the current `code-review-performance.md` lists only 4 generic patterns and the 7 missing ones appear nowhere in the codebase.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit instruction to `code-review-performance.md` directing the LLM to record 'breaks-under-load' / 'worse-with-scale' reasoning as evidence strings (not as severity), with an example of the expected format.
findings.finding: The 'bright-line reasoning' protocol (breaks-under-load / worse-with-scale recorded as EVIDENCE for Pass-2) is a processing/decision rule referenced in the plan but has no co-located instruction in the overlay prompt — `code-review-performance.md` contains no instruction telling the LLM to apply this reasoning or how to format it in the evidence field.
findings.location: src/rebar/llm/reviewers/code-review-performance.md / Plan: What section ('Bright-line reasoning…recorded as EVIDENCE for Pass-2 to score')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a bullet to the overlay's instruction block: `- For load-sensitive concerns, record your load-scaling rationale as an evidence string (e.g. "breaks-under-load: this query runs per-item in a loop") — never as a severity rating.`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the eval spec file path (e.g. `src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/code-review-performance.eval.yaml`), the required scorers (recall + no-fire deterministic), and the minimum fixture count (recall cases covering each of the 8 patterns; no-fire cases covering each FP guard).
findings.finding: The eval spec + fixtures file for the performance overlay is named as a required deliverable and underpins AC-2 ('Lands with a passing recall + no-fire eval'), but no such file exists anywhere in the repository and the plan specifies no file path, no fixture schema, and no eval structure for it.
findings.location: Plan: Files section ('eval spec + fixtures for the performance overlay (recall + no-fire cases)') / AC-2

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'passing recall + no-fire eval; does not over-suppress valid patterns' with a measurable threshold (e.g., '≥7 of 8 recall patterns fired across fixtures; ≤1 no-fire case incorrectly suppressed').
findings.finding: AC2's pass threshold is unstated: 'passing recall' and 'does not over-suppress valid patterns' are subjective without a numeric recall rate and a maximum false-negative/false-positive count.
findings.location: AC line 2: 'Lands with a passing recall + no-fire eval; does not over-suppress valid patterns'
findings.suggested_fix: AC2: 'Eval suite passes: ≥7/8 recall patterns trigger on at least one positive fixture each; 0 of N no-fire fixtures produce a false positive; eval is runnable via `make eval-performance` with a green exit code.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the concrete file path for the new eval spec to the Files section (expected: `src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/code-review-performance.eval.yaml`) so the edit target is fully enumerated and the eval CI can discover it.
findings.finding: The plan's second deliverable, 'eval spec + fixtures for the performance overlay,' names no concrete file path. No code-review-performance.eval.yaml exists anywhere in the repo (confirmed by search of src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/). This is a net-new file CREATION with an unspecified target path, making it an under-enumerated edit target.
findings.location: Files section: 'eval spec + fixtures for the performance overlay'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/code-review-performance.eval.yaml` as an explicit entry in the Files section, mirroring the convention used by `code-quality.eval.yaml` and `spec-alignment.eval.yaml`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete file path and prompt-id for the new eval spec (e.g., src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/code-review-performance.eval.yaml with prompt: code-review-performance) and note that the offline test gate must parametrize over it (mirroring the pattern in test_new_eval_specs.py).
findings.finding: The plan's eval deliverable ('eval spec + fixtures for the performance overlay') specifies no file path or prompt-id key; the eval loading mechanism (rebar.llm.evals.eval.load_eval_spec) resolves specs by exact prompt-id via a naming convention (src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/<prompt-id>.eval.yaml), so a mis-named spec would be silently undiscovered by the offline test gate.
findings.location: Files section — 'eval spec + fixtures for the performance overlay'

findings.finding: No file_impact declared on a leaf work ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Record the {path, reason} files this work will touch (e.g. via set_file_impact) so the attestation is scoped to them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact output path for the performance overlay eval spec (e.g. `src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/code-review-performance.eval.yaml`) in the Files section.
findings.finding: The plan's second deliverable ('eval spec + fixtures for the performance overlay') implies an eval spec file will be created, but no such file exists in `src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/` (confirmed: only plan-review, code-quality, completion-verifier, spec-alignment, ticket-quality evals are present). The plan names no concrete output path for this file.
findings.location: ## Files — 'eval spec + fixtures for the performance overlay (recall + no-fire cases)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/code-review-performance.eval.yaml` to the Files list, following the pattern of `src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/code-quality.eval.yaml`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Normalize terminology: pick one name ('FP-guards' or 'FP-discrimination guards') and use it consistently in both the What section and AC 1.
findings.finding: The plan uses 'FP-guards' in the What section and 'FP-discrimination guards' in AC 1 to name the same concept, introducing minor terminology drift that could cause implementer confusion about whether these are the same artifact.
findings.location: What section ('FP-guards') vs Acceptance Criteria AC 1 ('FP-discrimination guards')
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'FP-discrimination guards' in AC 1 with 'FP-guards' (or vice versa) so the criterion's verify step names the exact same entity described in the What section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-sentence positive rationale for the overlay-vs-verifier boundary (e.g., 'the Pass-2 verifier is intentionally domain-agnostic — it re-grounds evidence, not re-applies rubric — so performance-specific patterns belong only in the overlay finder') so AC3 is checkable without consulting review-kernel.md.
findings.finding: The plan gives no positive rationale for why the 'locked principle' (domain rubric in the overlay, not the verifier) is the right architectural choice — it asserts the principle as a given rather than grounding it in a codebase property.
findings.location: What section — 'per the locked principle that domain rubric lives in the overlay, not the generic Pass-2 verifier'
findings.suggested_fix: The Pass-2 verifier (code-review-verify.md) re-grounds evidence records against the diff using domain-agnostic binary sub-questions; it never re-applies the domain rubric. Performance-specific patterns in the verifier would duplicate the rubric and break the finder/verifier separation the kernel enforces.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a proving command for the recall + no-fire eval (e.g., `pytest tests/eval/performance/` or `make eval-performance`) so AC2 has a deterministic in-session check.
findings.finding: No concrete proving command is described for running the recall + no-fire eval; the plan names an 'eval spec + fixtures' file but gives no runner, CLI invocation, or toolchain.
findings.location: Files list: 'eval spec + fixtures for the performance overlay' / AC line 2: 'Lands with a passing recall + no-fire eval'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback path for the overlay enrichment (e.g., 'revert to prior overlay content via git revert' or 'maintain a versioned backup of the pre-enrichment overlay') so the plan satisfies T4's reversibility requirement.
findings.finding: The plan enriches an existing overlay file (changing the behavioral output of the performance reviewer) without stating a rollback path or back-out mechanism if the new FP-guards over-suppress currently-firing patterns in production.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-line rollback note under the Files section or AC 2: e.g., 'Rollback: revert src/rebar/llm/reviewers/code-review-performance.md to its pre-enrichment state via version control if recall or no-fire eval degrades post-merge.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
